title: Multipartite reference-frame-independent quantum cryptographic communication
authors: Donghwa Lee, Kyujin Shin, Hyang-Tag Lim, Yosep Kim, Yong-Su Kim
abstract: Reference frame mismatch among communication parties introduces errors in quantum cryptographic protocols. As the number of participants increases, aligning reference frames becomes increasingly difficult, complicating multipartite quantum cryptographic implementations. Here, we theoretically and experimentally investigate multipartite reference-frame-independent (RFI) quantum cryptographic communication using Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) states. We generalize the bipartite RFI security parameter $C$ to an $N$-party parameter $C_N$ and derive the asymptotic secret key rate expressed solely in terms of experimentally accessible quantities. We analyze the key rate under global and local depolarizing noise models and find that increasing the number of parties $N$ enhances robustness against global depolarizing noise while increasing vulnerability to local channel noise. We also present a proof-of-principle experimental demonstration of four-party RFI quantum cryptographic communication using four-photon GHZ states, confirming the reference-frame invariance of both the $C_4$ parameter and the secret key rate under various reference frame rotations.
summary: This paper theoretically and experimentally investigates Reference-Frame-Independent (RFI) Quantum Key Distribution using GHZ states across multiple parties. It generalizes the security analysis to N-parties, showing that while noise models affect robustness differently, the final secret key rate remains invariant under reference frame rotations. The work validates this concept with a four-party experimental demonstration.
main_problem: Multipartite quantum cryptographic communication is susceptible to errors caused by reference frame mismatches among participating parties, which complicates key distribution as the number of users increases.
main_result: The authors derived a generalized security parameter $C_N$ and demonstrated that while some metrics are sensitive to rotation, the asymptotic secret key rate ($R_N$) remains invariant under arbitrary reference frame rotations for N-party GHZ states.
method: The study combines theoretical derivation of key rates based on mutual information with experimental validation using four-photon GHZ states subjected to controlled reference frame rotations.
model_or_system: The system uses Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) states distributed among $N$ parties. The analysis models noise using global and local depolarizing channels, while the experiment utilizes photon sources based on PPKTP crystals.
key_observables: Multiparty RFI parameter ($C_N$), Quantum Bit Error Rates ($Q_Z$, $Q_X$), and the asymptotic secret key rate ($R_N$).
important_parameters: Number of parties ($N$), global/local depolarizing noise strengths ($\lambda_g, \lambda_l$), and reference frame rotation angles ($\gamma_i$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the ability to estimate noise parameters from measurement statistics in the X and Y bases; experimentally, it relies on generating high-fidelity GHZ states.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the setup with reference frame misalignments. Figure 2 compares key rate robustness under global vs. local noise for varying N. Figure 4 shows measured expectation values of Pauli strings and the invariance of $C_4$ and $R_4$ across different controlled rotations.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the problem of reference-frame mismatch in multipartite QKD, generalizes the security parameter to $N$ parties ($C_N$), derives the key rate formula under various noise models (global/local), and concludes with a proof-of-principle experiment demonstrating four-party RFI communication.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses robustness in quantum communication protocols, which is crucial for scaling up quantum networks. The mathematical generalization of security parameters to $N$ parties provides theoretical tools for future multi-user quantum internet applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
